message.content:
Here are example phrases and questions you can teach students. They’re framed to be:

- Open-ended rather than leading  
- Non‑graphic and neutral  
- Developmentally appropriate for a 15‑year‑old  
- Adaptable depending on what the young person has already shared  

You’d normally build up to the more specific ones after establishing rapport, confidentiality limits, and safety.

---

### General, gentle openings

- “Sometimes things happen in people’s lives that are hard to talk about, like being hurt or touched in ways they didn’t want. Has anything like that ever happened to you?”  
- “Has anyone ever done anything sexual with you, or asked you to do something sexual, that you didn’t want or weren’t comfortable with?”  
- “Sometimes young people are pressured into things they don’t really want to do. Has anyone ever pressured you into sexual things?”  

---

### Questions about physical contact/touching

- “Has anyone ever touched you in a way that made you feel uncomfortable, scared, or confused?”  
- “Has anyone ever touched your body in a way that you didn’t want them to?”  
- “Has anyone ever touched private parts of your body without your permission?”  
- “Has anyone ever asked you to touch their body in a way that didn’t feel okay to you?”  
- “Has anyone ever continued touching you even after you said no or tried to move away?”  

---

### Questions about consent and pressure

- “Have you ever felt like you couldn’t say no to something sexual, even if you wanted to?”  
- “Has anyone ever used threats, gifts, alcohol, drugs, or their position (like being older or in charge) to get you to do something sexual?”  
- “Have you ever gone along with sexual things because you were scared of what might happen if you didn’t?”  

---

### Questions about online/technology‑related contact

- “Has anyone ever sent you sexual messages or pictures that you didn’t want?”  
- “Has anyone ever asked you to send sexual pictures or videos of yourself?”  
- “Has anyone ever shared private or sexual pictures of you with other people, online or by phone, without your permission?”  

---

### Questions about specific people (non‑leading)

When there is a concern about someone in their environment:

- “Is there anyone in your life—at home, school, online, or in your community—who has made you feel unsafe or uncomfortable?”  
- “Have you ever felt unsafe or uncomfortable around an adult or older person because of the way they talked to you, looked at you, or touched you?”  
- “You mentioned [e.g., a family member/coach/partner]. Has anything ever happened with them that made you feel uncomfortable or not okay?”  

---

### Exploring after a partial disclosure (still non‑graphic)

If the adolescent indicates something has happened, keep questions open and simple:

- “Can you tell me a bit more about what happened, in your own words?”  
- “Where were you when that happened?”  
- “Do you remember what you were thinking or feeling at the time?”  
- “Has anything like that happened more than once?”  
- “Is the person who did this still in your life or near where you live or go to school?”  
- “Have you been worried that it might happen again?”  

---

### Questions about impact and safety

- “Since this happened, have you noticed any changes in your sleep, mood, school, or relationships?”  
- “Do you feel safe at home? At school? Online?”  
- “Is there anyone you feel safe with and can talk to about this?”  

---

### Phrasing to model for students

You can also teach them to normalize and give control:

- “You only need to share what you feel comfortable sharing today. I don’t need all the details to help you.”  
- “Some young people don’t have the words to describe what happened. You can explain it however feels right for you.”  
- “If I ask anything that feels too hard to answer, you can tell me and we can pause or skip it.”  

If you’d like, I can help you turn these into a one‑page handout or slide set with “Do/Don’t” examples (e.g., avoiding “Did someone rape you?” vs. using “Has anyone forced you into sexual activity?”).
